behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is availability check is skipped or broken in the query layer. A unit test of a pure filter function could pass while the actual query builder or ORM integration omits the availability WHERE clause. Must test: real product catalog with availability flags → recommendation engine → verify unavailable products absent from results.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Load test products with mixed availability states into a test database. Call recommendation endpoint for a user. Assert no out-of-stock products appear in results, even if they rank highly by other signals.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the blocking logic is not invoked or is incorrectly wired into the recommendation pipeline. A unit test of the filter function in isolation would not catch if the filtering step is skipped or if user preference data is stale. Must test: real user with blocked categories → recommendation engine → verify blocked categories absent.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Set up a test user with explicitly blocked product categories in their preferences. Call recommendation endpoint. Assert products from blocked categories do not appear in results, regardless of their algorithmic rank.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the ranking algorithm doesn't actually apply the browsing-pattern weight, or the weight is lower than expected. A pure unit test could pass while the actual algorithm ignores browsing history due to query issues or feature-engineering bugs. Must test: user with known browsing history → recommendation engine → verify products matching that history rank above unrelated products.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Create a user with clear browsing history in specific product categories. Load a product catalog where some items match browsing history and others don't. Call recommendation endpoint. Assert products matching browsing patterns appear in top positions; unrelated products appear lower.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: Pagination bugs (off-by-one errors, unstable sort, cursor corruption) often emerge from database query layer details, not pure logic. A unit test of offset/limit math could pass while the SQL ORDER BY produces inconsistent results or the database cursor drifts. Must test: real data → request page 2 twice → compare result sets for identical ordering and content.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Load test products into a database. Request page 2 of recommendations twice with identical parameters. Assert both responses have the same product IDs in the same order. Repeat with different page numbers to verify pagination correctness across the range.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the serendipity selector is never invoked or doesn't work. A unit test of the selector logic in isolation misses whether it's integrated into the pipeline or whether the algorithm correctly identifies unbrowsed categories. Must test: user with browsing history in subset of categories → recommendation engine → verify results include at least one product from an unbrowsed category.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Create a user who has only browsed categories A, B, C. Load products spanning categories A–F. Call recommendation endpoint. Assert at least one product from D, E, or F appears in results to meet serendipity requirement.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the fallback-to-popularity codepath is not triggered or returns empty results. A unit test of fallback logic could pass while the actual code never reaches the fallback due to query bugs or exception handling. Must test: new user profile (no history) → recommendation engine → verify non-empty results ranked by popularity, not personalization.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Create a brand-new user with zero purchase and browsing history. Load products with known popularity scores. Call recommendation endpoint. Assert non-empty results appear, ranked by popularity (verifiable by comparing to global product rankings).

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is click data is not recorded, is recorded with wrong values, or is recorded for wrong products due to race conditions or list reordering. A unit test of the tracking function in isolation misses integration bugs (wrong product ID extracted from list, race with cache invalidation). Must test: user clicks a recommendation → tracking system records → verify recorded product ID, position, timestamp are correct.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Get recommendations for a user. Simulate a click on product at position 3. Query the click tracking database/log. Assert recorded: (correct product_id, position=3, timestamp ≈ now). Repeat for different positions to verify correct mapping.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: Performance under scale requires real data volume and real algorithm execution. In-memory mocks and small test datasets hide O(n²) bugs, unoptimized queries, and memory bloat. Mocks execute orders of magnitude faster than real DB queries. Must test: load 100K products into real/production-like database → measure actual end-to-end recommendation latency → verify <200ms.
behaviors.test_description: System test: Populate a test/staging database with 100,000 real-scale products. Call recommendation endpoint multiple times (warm cache). Measure P95 latency over 100 requests. Assert all complete in <200ms. Profile to identify any O(n) or O(n²) bottlenecks.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the deprioritization logic doesn't fire or is overridden by stronger signals. A unit test of the rank-adjustment function could pass while the actual algorithm never applies it because user purchase state is stale or the ranking pipeline skips the step. Must test: user receives recommendations → user purchases one → request recommendations again → verify purchased product ranks lower or disappears.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Get recommendations for a user. Simulate user purchase of product from the list. Call recommendation endpoint again with updated user profile. Assert the purchased product either does not appear or ranks significantly lower than before purchase.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the algorithm contains hidden randomness (random sampling, unstable sort, cache races) that produces different results. A unit test of algorithm logic could pass while the actual pipeline introduces randomness via external data sources, concurrent updates, or non-deterministic library calls. Must test: same user state → request recommendations twice → verify identical ranked results (same product IDs in same order).
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Request recommendations for a user. Immediately request again with no intervening changes to user profile or catalog. Assert both responses return products in identical order. Repeat 5+ times to catch sporadic non-determinism.
